name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَىْءٍ حَتَّىٰ تُقِيمُوا۟ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكُم مِّن رَّبِّكُمْ ۗ وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُم مَّآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِن رَّبِّكَ طُغْيَـٰنًا وَكُفْرًا ۖ فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٦٨)
```

```english
Say, ‘O People of the Book! You do not stand on anything until you observe the Torah and the Evangel and what was sent down to you from your Lord.’ Surely many of them will be increased in rebellion and unfaith by what has been sent down to you from your Lord. So do not grieve for the faithless lot. (68)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَٱلَّذِينَ هَادُوا۟ وَٱلصَّـٰبِـُٔونَ وَٱلنَّصَـٰرَىٰ مَنْ ءَامَنَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلْيَوْمِ ٱلْـَٔاخِرِ وَعَمِلَ صَـٰلِحًا فَلَا خَوْفٌ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا هُمْ يَحْزَنُونَ (٦٩)
```

```english
Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Sabaeans, and the Christians—those who have faith in Allah and the Last Day and act righteously—they will have no fear, nor will they grieve. (69)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَـٰقَ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَأَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَيْهِمْ رُسُلًا ۖ كُلَّمَا جَآءَهُمْ رَسُولٌۢ بِمَا لَا تَهْوَىٰٓ أَنفُسُهُمْ فَرِيقًا كَذَّبُوا۟ وَفَرِيقًا يَقْتُلُونَ (٧٠)
```

```english
Certainly We took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We sent apostles to them. Whenever an apostle brought them that which was not to their liking, they would impugn a part of them, and a part they would slay. (70)
```

```arabic
وَحَسِبُوٓا۟ أَلَّا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ فَعَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ ثُمَّ تَابَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ عَمُوا۟ وَصَمُّوا۟ كَثِيرٌ مِّنْهُمْ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ (٧١)
```

```english
They supposed there would be no testing, so they became blind and deaf. Thereafter Allah accepted their repentance, yet [again] many of them became blind and deaf, and Allah watches what they do. (71)
```

```arabic
لَقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ هُوَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ ۖ وَقَالَ ٱلْمَسِيحُ يَـٰبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ رَبِّى وَرَبَّكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُۥ مَن يُشْرِكْ بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ ٱلْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَىٰهُ ٱلنَّارُ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّـٰلِمِينَ مِنْ أَنصَارٍ (٧٢)
```

```english
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary.’ But the Messiah had said, ‘O Children of Israel! Worship Allah, my Lord, and your Lord. Indeed whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entry into] paradise, and his refuge shall be the Fire, and the wrongdoers will not have any helpers.’ (72)
```

```arabic
لَّقَدْ كَفَرَ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَـٰثَةٍ ۘ وَمَا مِنْ إِلَـٰهٍ إِلَّآ إِلَـٰهٌ وَٰحِدٌ ۚ وَإِن لَّمْ يَنتَهُوا۟ عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (٧٣)
```

```english
They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity,’ while there is no god except the One God. If they do not relinquish what they say, there shall befall the faithless among them a painful punishment. (73)
```

```arabic
أَفَلَا يَتُوبُونَ إِلَى ٱللَّهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَهُۥ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (٧٤)
```

```english
Will they not repent to Allah and plead to Him for forgiveness? Yet Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful. (74)
```

```arabic
مَّا ٱلْمَسِيحُ ٱبْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِن قَبْلِهِ ٱلرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُۥ صِدِّيقَةٌ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ ٱلطَّعَامَ ۗ ٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ ٱلْـَٔايَـٰتِ ثُمَّ ٱنظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ (٧٥)
```

```english
The Messiah, son of Mary, is but an apostle. Certainly, [other] apostles have passed before him, and his mother was a truthful one. Both of them would eat food. Look how We clarify the signs for them, and yet, look, how they go astray! (75)
```

```arabic
قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا ۚ وَٱللَّهُ هُوَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٧٦)
```

```english
Say, ‘Do you worship, besides Allah, what has no power to bring you any benefit or harm, while Allah—He is the All-hearing, the All-knowing?!’ (76)
```

```arabic
قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لَا تَغْلُوا۟ فِى دِينِكُمْ غَيْرَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوٓا۟ أَهْوَآءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوا۟ مِن قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا۟ كَثِيرًا وَضَلُّوا۟ عَن سَوَآءِ ٱلسَّبِيلِ (٧٧)
```

```english
Say, ‘O People of the Book! Do not unduly exceed the bounds in your religion and do not follow the fancies of a people who went astray in the past, and led many astray, and [themselves] strayed from the right path.’ (77)
```

```arabic
لُعِنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ مِنۢ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ عَلَىٰ لِسَانِ دَاوُۥدَ وَعِيسَى ٱبْنِ مَرْيَمَ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِمَا عَصَوا۟ وَّكَانُوا۟ يَعْتَدُونَ (٧٨)
```

```english
The faithless among the Children of Israel were cursed on the tongue of David and Jesus son of Mary. That, because they would disobey and they used to commit transgression. (78)
```

```arabic
كَانُوا۟ لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ عَن مُّنكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا كَانُوا۟ يَفْعَلُونَ (٧٩)
```

```english
They would not forbid one another from the wrongs that they committed. Surely, evil is what they had been doing. (79)
```

```arabic
تَرَىٰ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ ۚ لَبِئْسَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ لَهُمْ أَنفُسُهُمْ أَن سَخِطَ ٱللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَفِى ٱلْعَذَابِ هُمْ خَـٰلِدُونَ (٨٠)
```

```english
You see many of them fraternizing with the faithless. Surely evil is what they have sent ahead for their souls, as Allah is displeased with them and they shall remain in punishment [forever]. (80)
```

```arabic
وَلَوْ كَانُوا۟ يُؤْمِنُونَ بِٱللَّهِ وَٱلنَّبِىِّ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَا ٱتَّخَذُوهُمْ أَوْلِيَآءَ وَلَـٰكِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنْهُمْ فَـٰسِقُونَ (٨١)
```

```english
Had they believed in Allah and the Prophet and what has been sent down to him, they would not have taken them for allies. But most of them are transgressors. (81)
```

```arabic
۞ لَتَجِدَنَّ أَشَدَّ ٱلنَّاسِ عَدَٰوَةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلْيَهُودَ وَٱلَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا۟ ۖ وَلَتَجِدَنَّ أَقْرَبَهُم مَّوَدَّةً لِّلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱلَّذِينَ قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّا نَصَـٰرَىٰ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ وَرُهْبَانًا وَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ (٨٢)
```

```english
Surely You will find the most hostile of all people towards the faithful to be the Jews and the polytheists, and surely you will find the nearest of them in affection to the faithful to be those who say ‘We are Christians.’ That is because there are priests and monks among them, and because they are not arrogant. (82)
```

```arabic
وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا۟ مَآ أُنزِلَ إِلَى ٱلرَّسُولِ تَرَىٰٓ أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ ٱلدَّمْعِ مِمَّا عَرَفُوا۟ مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ ۖ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱكْتُبْنَا مَعَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ (٨٣)
```

```english
When they hear what has been revealed to the Apostle, you see their eyes fill with tears because of the truth that they recognize. They say, ‘Our Lord, we believe; so write us down among the witnesses. (83)
```

```arabic
وَمَا لَنَا لَا نُؤْمِنُ بِٱللَّهِ وَمَا جَآءَنَا مِنَ ٱلْحَقِّ وَنَطْمَعُ أَن يُدْخِلَنَا رَبُّنَا مَعَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (٨٤)
```

```english
Why should we not believe in Allah and the truth that has come to us, eager as we are that our Lord should admit us among the righteous people?’ (84)
```

```arabic
فَأَثَـٰبَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِمَا قَالُوا۟ جَنَّـٰتٍ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا ۚ وَذَٰلِكَ جَزَآءُ ٱلْمُحْسِنِينَ (٨٥)
```

```english
So, for what they said, Allah requited them with gardens with streams running in them, to remain in them [forever], and that is the reward of the virtuous. (85)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَكَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ أَصْحَـٰبُ ٱلْجَحِيمِ (٨٦)
```

```english
But those who are faithless and deny Our signs—they shall be the inmates of hell. (86)
```

## (Commentary)

In themselves, the verses are capable of connection and consistency according to their arrangement, yet they do not accept connection with His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَوْ أَنَّهُمْ أَقَامُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ** — "Had they observed the Torah and the Evangel" (5:66), if one disregards His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ** — "O Apostle! Communicate that which has been sent down to you from your Lord" (5:67). As for the link regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يَا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ** — "O Apostle! Communicate," you have already encountered the discussion concerning it.

It is most likely that these verses follow the context of the preceding verses from the beginning of the Surah until here—meaning the interconnection of the themes of the verses starting from His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَقَدْ أَخَذَ اللَّهُ مِيثَاقَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَبَعَثْنَا مِنْهُمْ اثْنَيْ عَشَرَ نَقِيبًا** — "Certainly Allah took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We raised among them twelve chiefs" (5:12) until the end of these verses under discussion, with the exception of a few interspersed verses such as the Verse of Mastership (آية الولاية) and the Verse of Communication (آية التبليغ) and others previously discussed. The same applies to the connection of the verses at the end of the Surah with these verses, for they are all united by being discourse related to the state of the People of the Book (أهل الكتاب).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ حَتَّىٰ تُقِيمُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! You do not stand on anything until you observe the Torah and the Evangel’" (5:68) until the end of the verse. A human being finds within himself during his actions that if he wishes to exert power and intensity in a matter that requires it, he must rely on a level surface upon which he stands firm or to which he is connected. For instance, one who wishes to pull, push, carry, or lift a heavy object first firms his feet upon the ground and then does as he pleases, knowing that were it not for this, he would not achieve his aim. This has been discussed in the sciences related to it.

If we apply this meaning to spiritual matters, such as the soul's internal actions or the physical actions of the limbs related to psychological states, it results in the conclusion that the performance of significant deeds and great works depends upon a spiritual foundation (أس معنوي) and a strong psychological base (مبنى قوى نفسي). This is similar to how momentous affairs depend on patience (الصبر), steadfastness (الثبات), high ambition (علو الهمة), and strength of resolve (قوة العزيمة), and how success in servitude (العبودية) depends on the reality of godwariness (حق التقوى) and piety (الورع) regarding the prohibitions of Allah.

From here, it becomes clear that His saying, the Exalted: **لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ** — "You do not stand on anything" is a metaphor (كناية) for their lack of reliance on anything upon which they could firm their feet so as to be able to observe the Torah, the Evangel, and what was sent down to them from their Lord. It is an allusion (تلويح) to the fact that the religion (الدين) of Allah and His judgment possess a weight (ثقل) that is not easily carried by a human until he relies on a firm foundation; he cannot establish it merely through his own whims (هوى). Allah points to this regarding the Noble Quran with His saying: **إِنَّا سَنُلْقِي عَلَيْكَ قَوْلًا ثَقِيلًا** — "Soon We shall cast on you a weighty word" (73:5), and His saying: **لَوْ أَنْزَلْنَا هَٰذَا الْقُرْآنَ عَلَىٰ جَبَلٍ لَرَأَيْتَهُ خَاشِعًا مُتَصَدِّعًا مِنْ خَشْيَةِ اللَّهِ ۚ وَتِلْكَ الْأَمْثَالُ نَضْرِبُهَا لِلنَّاسِ لَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَفَكَّرُونَ** — "Had We sent down this Quran upon a mountain, you would have seen it humbled [and] rent asunder out of the fear of Allah. We coin such similitudes for mankind so that they may reflect" (59:21), and His saying: **إِنَّا عَرَضْنَا الْأَمَانَةَ عَلَى السَّمَاوَاتِ وَالْأَرْضِ وَالْجِبَالِ فَأَبَيْنَ أَنْ يَحْمِلْنَهَا وَأَشْفَقْنَ مِنْهَا** — "Indeed We presented the Trust to the heavens and the earth and the mountains, but they refused to bear it, and were apprehensive of it" (33:72).

Regarding the matter of the Torah, He said, addressing Moses (عليه السلام): **فَخُذْهَا بِقُوَّةٍ وَأْمُرْ قَوْمَكَ يَأْخُذُوا بِأَحْسَنِهَا** — "So hold on to them with power, and bid your people to hold on to the best of them" (7:145). He said, addressing the Children of Israel: **خُذُوا مَا آتَيْنَاكُمْ بِقُوَّةٍ** — "Hold on with power to what We have given you" (2:63). And He said, addressing John (عليه السلام): **يَا يَحْيَىٰ خُذِ الْكِتَابَ بِقُوَّةٍ** — "O John! Hold on to the Book with power" (19:12).

Thus, the meaning returns to this: you lack the support (العماد) upon which you must rely to establish the religion of Allah sent down to you in His books—which is godwariness (التقوى) and turning to Allah in repentance (الإنابة) by returning to Him time and again, connecting with Him, and seeking refuge in His pillar. Instead, you are arrogant toward His obedience and transgressors of His limits.

This meaning is evident from His saying, the Exalted, addressing His Prophet and the believers: **شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِ نُوحًا وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنَا إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِ إِبْرَاهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ** — "He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus" (42:13). He gathered the entirety of the religion in what He mentioned, then said: **أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** — "‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it’" (42:13). He clarified that all of this returns to establishing the religion as a single word without division. Then He said: **كَبُرَ عَلَى الْمُشْرِكِينَ مَا تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ** — "Hard on the polytheists is that to which you summon them" (42:13), and that is due to the difficulty of agreement and uprightness (الاستقامة) in following the religion for them. Then He said: **اللَّهُ يَجْتَبِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ يَشَاءُ وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ يُنِيبُ** — "Allah chooses for it whomever He wishes and He guides to it whomever returns [to Him] penitently" (42:13). He informed that establishing the religion is not possible except through guidance (الهدى) from Allah, and none are fit for it except those characterized by penitence (الإنابة)—which is the connection with Allah and not being severed from Him by returning to Him time and again. Then He said: **وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ** — "They did not divide [into sects] except after the knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (42:14). He mentioned that the reason for their division and failure to establish the religion was their rebellion (البغي) and transgression from the middle path of justice (الوسط العدل) set for them.

He also said in a similar verse: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا ۚ فِطْرَتَ اللَّهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْهَا ۚ لَا تَبْدِيلَ لِخَلْقِ اللَّهِ ۚ ذَٰلِكَ الدِّينُ الْقَيِّمُ وَلَٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَ النَّاسِ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ * مُنِيبِينَ إِلَيْهِ وَاتَّقُوهُ وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ وَلَا تَكُونُوا مِنَ الْمُشْرِكِينَ * مِنَ الَّذِينَ فَرَّقُوا دِينَهُمْ وَكَانُوا شِيَعًا ۖ كُلُّ حِزْبٍ بِمَا لَدَيْهِمْ فَرِحُونَ** — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith, the nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind (there is no altering Allah’s creation; that is the upright religion, but most people do not know). Turning penitently to Him, and be wary of Him and maintain the prayer, and do not be among the polytheists—of those who split up their religion and became sects, each party exulting in what it had" (30:30-32). He mentioned here as well that the means to establish the religion of primordial nature (دين الفطرة) is penitence (الإنابة) toward Allah, maintaining the connection with His presence, and not being severed from His cause.

He alluded to this reality in the verses preceding these verses under discussion as well, where He mentioned that Allah cursed the Jews and was angry with them for transgressing His limits, so He cast enmity and hatred among them. He mentioned this meaning in another place specifically regarding the Christians with His saying: **فَأَغْرَيْنَا بَيْنَهُمُ الْعَدَاوَةَ وَالْبَغْضَاءَ إِلَىٰ يَوْمِ الْقِيَامَةِ** — "So We stirred up enmity and hatred among them until the Day of Resurrection" (5:14).

Allah, the Glorified, warned the Muslims against such a painful calamity that He would bring upon the People of the Book from the Jews and Christians, and He informed them that it is not possible, nor will it ever be possible, for them to establish the Torah, the Evangel, and what was sent down to them from their Lord [without this foundation]. The course of history has confirmed what the Book informed regarding the fragmentation of sects among them and the casting of enmity and hatred between them. Thus, He warned the Islamic nation (الأمة الإسلامية) against entering their path of being severed from their Lord and failing to turn to Him in penitence, in His saying: **فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفًا** — "So set your heart on the religion as a people of pure faith" (30:30) in several verses of the Surah.

The discussion of some verses alluding to this has already passed in previous parts of the book, and the discussion of others will come, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

As for His saying, the Exalted: **وَلَيَزِيدَنَّ كَثِيرًا مِنْهُمْ مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ طُغْيَانًا وَكُفْرًا** — "What has been sent down to you from your Lord will surely increase many of them in rebellion and unfaith" (5:68), the discussion of its meaning has already passed. His saying: **فَلَا تَأْسَ عَلَى الْقَوْمِ الْكَافِرِينَ** — "So do not grieve for the faithless people" (5:68) is a consolation from Him, the Exalted, to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) in the form of a prohibition against grieving.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا وَالصَّابِئُونَ وَالنَّصَارَىٰ** — "Indeed the faithful, the Jews, the Sabaeans, and the Christians" (5:69). Its apparent meaning is that **الصَّابِئُونَ** (the Sabaeans) is conjoined (عطف) to **الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** (the faithful) according to its position. A group of grammarians prohibit conjoining to the noun of *inna* (اسم إن) in the nominative case (بالرفع) before the predicate (الخبر) has passed, but the verse is an argument against them.

The verse is in the position of clarifying that in the matter of felicity (السعادة), no weight is given to names and titles—such as a group calling themselves "the believers" (المؤمنين), a sect "the Jews" (الذين هادوا), a party "the Sabaeans" (الصابئين), and others "the Christians" (النصارى). Rather, the weight is given to faith (الإيمان) in Allah and the Last Day and righteous deeds (العمل الصالح). The discussion of the meaning of the verse has already passed in the exegesis of Surah al-Baqarah, verse 62, in the first volume of the book.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَدْ أَخَذْنَا مِيثَاقَ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ وَأَرْسَلْنَا إِلَيْهِمْ رُسُلًا** — "Certainly We took a pledge from the Children of Israel, and We sent apostles to them" (5:70) until the end of the verse. This verse and those following it for several verses address the state of the People of the Book as an argument for what is contained in His saying, the Exalted: **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ حَتَّىٰ تُقِيمُوا التَّوْرَاةَ وَالْإِنْجِيلَ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! You do not stand on anything until you observe the Torah and the Evangel’" (5:68). For these crimes and sins do not leave a person any connection with his Lord such that he could establish the books of Allah while relying upon Him.

It is possible that the verses are linked to His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا** — "Indeed the faithful, the Jews" (5:69), thus being a confirmation that names and titles benefit nothing in the stage of felicity; for if they benefited, they would have prevented these people from killing the prophets, denying them, and perishing through the destroyers of tribulations (الفتن) and the ruinous sins (موبقات الذنوب).

It is also possible that these verses serve as an explanation (المبينة) for His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا** (5:69), which itself is like an explanation for His saying: **يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَسْتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَيْءٍ** (5:68), and the meaning is clear.

His saying: **فَرِيقًا كَذَّبُوا وَفَرِيقًا يَقْتُلُونَ** — "some they would impugn and some they would slay" (5:70). It appears that the word **فَرِيقًا** (a group) in both places are objects (مفعولان) for the two verbs following them, placed before them for emphasis on their matter. The estimation is: "they denied a group and they kill a group." The whole is the response to His saying: **كُلَّمَا جَاءَهُمْ** — "Whenever [there came to them]" (5:70). The meaning is similar to our saying: whenever a messenger came to them with what their souls did not desire, they mistreated his encounter and response, and they divided the coming messengers into two groups: a group they denied and a group they kill.

It is said in *al-Majma‘*: "If it is asked: why was the future tense conjoined to the past—meaning in His saying: **فَرِيقًا كَذَّبُوا وَفَرِيقًا يَقْتُلُونَ**? The answer is: to indicate that this is their habitual character (شأنهم); thus it contains the meaning that they denied and killed, and they deny and kill, in addition to the fact that His saying **يَقْتُلُونَ** (they kill) must originally be in agreement with the verse endings (رءوس الآي)." End quote.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَحَسِبُوا أَلَّا تَكُونَ فِتْنَةٌ فَعَمُوا وَصَمُّوا** — "They supposed there would be no testing, so they became blind and deaf" (5:71) completes the discourse of the previous verse. Supposition (الحسبان) is an opinion (الظن), and testing (الفتنة) is the trial (المحنة) that deludes a person, or it is more general than every evil and calamity. Blindness (العمى) is the failure to perceive the truth and the failure to distinguish good from evil. Deafness (الصمم) is the failure to hear exhortation and the failure to heed advice. This blindness and deafness are the effects of their supposition that there would be no testing. It appears that their supposition of this was the effect of the dignity (الكرامة) they estimated for themselves by being from the tribe of Israel and that they are the children of Allah and His beloved ones, so no evil would touch them regardless of what they did or committed.

So the meaning of the verse—and Allah knows best—is that because of what they believed for themselves regarding the dignity of being Jewish, they thought that no evil would strike them or that they would not be tested for what they did. That thought and supposition blinded their eyes from seeing the truth and deafened their ears from hearing what would benefit them from the call of their prophets.

This is among the things that favor our suggestion that the verses are like an explanatory argument for His saying: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَالَّذِينَ هَادُوا** (5:69). The sum of the meaning is that names and titles benefit no one at all; for these Jews were not benefited by the dignity they estimated for themselves through the name, but rather it blinded them and led them to the place of destruction and testing when they denied the prophets of Allah and killed them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **ثُمَّ تَابَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ ثُمَّ عَمُوا وَصَمُّوا كَثِيرٌ مِنْهُمْ ۚ وَاللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "Then Allah turned to them clemently, again they became blind and deaf—many of them; and Allah sees best what they do" (5:71). Repentance (التوبة) from Allah toward His servants is His return to them with mercy. This indicates that Allah, the Glorified, had distanced them from His mercy and care, and therefore the aforementioned supposition took hold of them and blindness and deafness became necessary for them. However, Allah, the Glorified, returned to them a second time with repentance, so He lifted this supposition from their hearts, and the blindness and deafness from their eyes and ears. Thus, they recognized themselves as servants who have no dignity before Allah except through godwariness (التقوى), and they saw the truth and heard the exhortation of Allah to them through the tongues of His prophets, so it became clear to them that the naming benefits nothing.

"Then they became blind and deaf—many of them." The attribution of blindness and deafness to their collective first, and then to many of them—by bringing "many of them" as a substitute (بدل) for the plural pronoun—is an act of fairness in speech. It indicates that the attribution of blindness and deafness to their collective is of the type of attributing the ruling of the part to the whole. In reality, those characterized by these two attributes were many of them, not all of them, first. Secondly, it hints that the blindness and deafness mentioned first encompassed all of them, as indicated by the contrast second. Thirdly, it indicates that the divine repentance did not have its effect nullified nor did it go to waste entirely; rather, some of them were saved by the repentance and were not overtaken by the subsequent blindness and deafness.

Then He, the Exalted, concluded the verse with His saying: **وَاللَّهُ بَصِيرٌ بِمَا يَعْمَلُونَ** — "and Allah sees best what they do" to indicate that nothing escapes Allah's notice. Others, when they honor a people with dignity, it places a veil over their sight preventing them from seeing their evil and disliked deeds. Allah, the Glorified, is not of this description; rather, He is the All-Seeing (البصير) whom nothing veils from anything.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ هُوَ الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ** — "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary’" (5:72). This is like a clarification that Christianity and the affiliation with the Messiah (عليه السلام) did not benefit the Christians against the attachment of disbelief (الكفر) to them, since they associated partners with Allah and did not believe in Him with His true faith when they said: "Indeed Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary."

The Christians, although they differed in the manner of the Messiah, son of Mary, encompassing the essence of divinity (جوهرة الألوهية)—between those who say the hypostasis (أقنوم) of the Messiah, which is Knowledge, is derived from the hypostasis of the Lord (the Exalted), which is Life, and that is Fatherhood and Sonship; and those who say that He, the Exalted, became the Messiah by way of transformation (الانقلاب); and those who say He indwelt (حل) him, as the detailed clarification of this has already passed in the discussion on Jesus, son of Mary (عليه السلام), in the exegesis of Surah Al-Imran in the third volume of the book.

However, all three views are capable of being applied to this statement ("that Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary"). Thus, it appears that the intended meaning of those who uttered this statement is all Christians who exaggerate (الغالين) regarding the Messiah (عليه السلام), not specifically those among them who believe in transformation.

The description of the Messiah as "son of Mary" is not devoid of indication or hint toward the cause of their disbelief, which is the attribution of divinity to a human, the son of a human, both created from dust. And where is dust compared to the Lord of Lords?!

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَقَالَ الْمَسِيحُ يَا بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ اعْبُدُوا اللَّهَ رَبِّي وَرَبَّكُمْ** — "But the Messiah said, ‘O Children of Israel! Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord’" (5:72) until the end of the verse. This is an argument against their disbelief and the falsehood of their statement using the words of the Messiah (عليه السلام) himself. For his saying (عليه السلام): "Worship Allah, my Lord and your Lord" indicates that he is a servant under lordship (عبد مربوب) like them. And his saying: **إِنَّهُ مَنْ يُشْرِكْ بِاللَّهِ فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ الْجَنَّةَ** — "Indeed whoever ascribes partners to Allah, Allah shall forbid him [entry into] paradise" (5:72) indicates that whoever makes a partner for Allah in His divinity is a polytheist (مشرك), a disbeliever (كافر) for whom Paradise is forbidden.

In His saying, the Exalted, narrating from him (عليه السلام): **فَقَدْ حَرَّمَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِ الْجَنَّةَ وَمَأْوَاهُ النَّارُ ۖ وَمَا لِلظَّالِمِينَ مِنْ أَنْصَارٍ** — "Allah shall forbid him [entry into] paradise, and his abode shall be the Fire, and the wrongdoers shall not have any helpers" (5:72), there is a specific concern with nullifying what they attribute to the Messiah regarding the narrative of atonement (التفدية)—that he (عليه السلام), by his choice of the crucifixion, ransomed them with himself, so they are forgiven, divine obligations are lifted from them, their destination is Paradise, and they shall not touch the Fire, as the report of this from them has already passed in the exegesis of Surah Al-Imran in the story of Jesus (عليه السلام). The story of atonement and the crucifixion was only advanced for this purpose.

What the verse narrates of his saying (عليه السلام) is found in scattered chapters of the Gospels, such as the command for monotheism (التوحيد), (1) the nullification of the worship of the polytheist, (2) and the judgment of the eternity of the wrongdoers in the Fire (3).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَاثَةٍ** — "They are certainly faithless who say, ‘Allah is the third [person] of a trinity’" (5:73). That is, one of the three: the Father, the Son, and the Spirit; meaning He applies to each one of the three. This is a necessary consequence of their saying: the Father is God, the Son is God, and the Spirit is God, and He is three, and He is one. They simulate by this something similar to our saying: "Zayd bin Amr is a human." There are three matters there: Zayd, bin Amr, and human; and there is one matter, which is the one described by these attributes. They have overlooked that if this multiplicity (الكثرة) is real and not conceptual (اعتبارية), it necessitates multiplicity in the described one in reality. And if the described one is one in reality, it necessitates that the multiplicity be conceptual and not real. Thus, the combination of this numerical multiplicity and numerical unity (الوحدة العددية) in Zayd, who is described as such in reality, is something the intellect recoils from conceptualizing.

Therefore, some Christian missionaries have mentioned that the issue of the Trinity (التثليث) is among the traditional matters from the doctrines of the predecessors that do not accept resolution according to scientific standards. He did not realize that he must demand proof for every claim that reaches his ears, whether it be from the claims of the predecessors or the claims of the successors.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَمَا مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِلَّا إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ** — "while there is no god except the One God" (5:73) until the end of the verse. This is a refutation from Him, the Exalted, of their saying: "Indeed Allah is the third of three," by stating that Allah, the Glorified, does not accept multiplicity in His transcendent essence in any way. He, the Exalted, is One in His essence. When He is described by His noble attributes and His beautiful names, that does not add anything to His single essence, nor does an attribute, when added to another attribute, result in multiplicity or plurality. He, the Exalted, is unique in essence (أحدي الذات), indivisible in external reality, in imagination, or in the intellect.

So Allah, the Glorified, is not such that He is partitioned in His essence into thing and thing at all, nor is His essence such that it is permissible for something to be added to Him so that He becomes two or more. How could it be so, when He, the Exalted, is with this thing that is intended to be added to Him, the Exalted, in imagination, supposition, or external reality?

He, the Exalted, is One in His essence, but not with the numerical unity (الوحدة العددية) that belongs to other things from which multiplicities are formed. Nor is He described by multiplicity in essence, name, or attribute. How could He be, when this numerical unity and the multiplicity composed of it are both from the effects of His making and creation? How then could He be described by what is from His own making?

In His saying, the Exalted: **وَمَا مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِلَّا إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ** — "while there is no god except the One God," there is an emphasis in establishing monotheism (التوحيد) that is not found in others, as the discourse is presented in the form of negation and exception. Then **مِنْ** was introduced into the negation to provide emphasis of exhaustiveness (تأكيد الاستغراق). Then the excepted (المستثنى) was brought, which is His saying: **إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ** (One God) in the indefinite form (بالتنكير) which signifies kind (التنويع). Had it been brought in the definite form, such as our saying "except the One God" (إلا الإله الواحد), it would not have provided what is intended of the reality of monotheism.

So the meaning is: "There is nothing in existence of the genus of god at all except a God who is one with a type of unity that does not accept plurality at all—neither plurality of essence nor plurality of attributes, neither externally nor by supposition." If it were said: "And there is no god except Allah the One," it would not have refuted the statement of the Christians ("that Allah is the third of three"), for they do not deny unity in Him, the Exalted; they only say: He is one essence that has determination through its three attributes, and He is one in the very same sense that He is truly many.

What they have supposed of the meaning is not refuted except by establishing a unity from which no multiplicity is formed at all, and that is what the Noble Quran aims for with His saying: **وَمَا مِنْ إِلَٰهٍ إِلَّا إِلَٰهٌ وَاحِدٌ**.

This is among the subtle meanings alluded to by the Divine Book regarding the reality of the meaning of monotheism (التوحيد). We shall delve into a comprehensive discussion of it in a specific Quranic study, then in an intellectual study and another traditional one, to fulfill its right.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِنْ لَمْ يَنْتَهُوا عَمَّا يَقُولُونَ لَيَمَسَّنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ** — "If they do not relinquish what they say, there shall strike the faithless among them a painful punishment" (5:73). This is a threat to them of the painful punishment of the Hereafter, which is the apparent meaning of the noble verse.

Since the statement of the Trinity (التثليث) contained in the phrase "Indeed Allah is the third of three" is not within the capacity of the intellects of the general public to conceptualize, most Christians receive it as a settled sectarian statement by word without conceptualizing its meaning, nor do they aspire to conceptualize it—just as it is not within the capacity of the sound intellect to conceptualize it correctly. It is only conceptualized like the conceptualization of impossible suppositions, such as "the human non-human," and "the number that is neither one nor many, nor even nor odd." Therefore, the general public accepts it as a reception without investigating its meaning; they only believe in sonship and fatherhood as a kind of honorific meaning. These people, in reality, are not from the people of the Trinity; they merely mouth the word and affiliate themselves with it. This is in contrast to those other than the general public among them, who are those to whom Allah, the Glorified, attributes the disagreement of sects and determines that it was due to their rebellion (بغيهم), as He, the Exalted, said: **أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ** — "‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided regarding it’" until He said: **وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ مَا جَاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْيًا بَيْنَهُمْ** — "They did not divide [into sects] except after the knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves" (42:13-14).

Thus, true disbelief (الكفر الحقيقي) that does not lead to being deemed oppressed (استضعاف)—which is that which contains the denial of monotheism and the rejection of the signs of Allah—is only completed in some of them, not all of them. Allah has only promised the eternal Fire to those who disbelieved and denied the signs of Allah; He said: **وَالَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا وَكَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا أُولَٰئِكَ أَصْحَابُ النَّارِ ۖ هُمْ فِيهَا خَالِدُونَ** — "As for those who are faithless and deny Our signs, they shall be the inmates of the Fire and they shall remain in it [forever]" (2:39), among other verses. The discussion on this has already passed in the exegesis of His saying, the Exalted: **إِلَّا الْمُسْتَضْعَفِينَ** — "except the oppressed" (4:98).

Perhaps this is the secret in the apparent partiality (التبعيض) in His saying: **لَيَمَسَّنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ** — "there shall strike the faithless among them." Or the intended meaning is to point out that among the Christians are those who do not believe in the Trinity and do not believe regarding the Messiah except that he is the servant of Allah and His messenger, as were the Christians of Abyssinia and others according to what history has recorded. So the meaning is: if the Christians do not relinquish what they say (attributing the statement of some of the group to all of them), there shall strike the faithless among them—who are those who say the Trinity among them—a painful punishment.

They have sometimes explained the discourse—meaning His saying: **لَيَمَسَّنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْهُمْ**—as being of the type of placing the manifest noun (الظاهر) in the place of the pronoun (المضمر), where the original would be: "there shall strike them." The shift to placing the relative pronoun (الموصول) and its relative clause (صلته) in its place was to indicate that this statement is disbelief in Allah, and that disbelief is the cause of the punishment He threatened them with.

This is a plausible explanation, were it not for the fact that the verse is prefaced by His saying: **لَقَدْ كَفَرَ الَّذِينَ قَالُوا إِنَّ اللَّهَ ثَالِثُ ثَلَاثَةٍ**. Similar to it in distance is the statement of another: that **مِنْ** (among) in His saying **مِنْهُمْ** (among them) is explanatory (بيانية), for it is a statement without proof.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَفَلَا يَتُوبُونَ إِلَى اللَّهِ وَيَسْتَغْفِرُونَهُ ۚ وَاللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَحِيمٌ** — "Will they not turn to Allah penitently and plead to Him for forgiveness? And Allah is all-forgiving, all-merciful" (5:74). This is an exhortation (تحضيض) toward repentance and seeking forgiveness, and a reminder of Allah's forgiveness and mercy, or it is a disapproval (إنكار) or a rebuke (توبيخ).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **مَا الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ إِلَّا رَسُولٌ قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ وَأُمُّهُ صِدِّيقَةٌ ۖ كَانَا يَأْكُلَانِ الطَّعَامَ** — "The Messiah, son of Mary, is but an apostle. Certainly [other] apostles have passed before him. His mother was a truthful woman. Both of them used to eat food" (5:75). This is a refutation of their saying: "Indeed Allah is the third of three," or of both this saying and their saying narrated in the previous verse: "Indeed Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary." Its summary is that the Messiah's encompassing of the essence of divinity is refuted by the fact that the Messiah does not differ from the rest of the messengers of Allah who passed away before him; they were humans sent as messengers without being lords besides Allah, the Glorified. Likewise, his mother Mary was a truthful woman (صِدِّيقَةٌ) who believed in the signs of Allah, the Exalted, and she was a human. Both he and his mother used to eat food. Eating food, with what follows it, is built upon the foundation of need (الحاجة), which is the first sign of contingency (الإمكان) and being a created thing (المصنوعية). Thus, the Messiah (عليه السلام) was a contingent being born from a contingent being, and a servant and a created messenger from his mother; they both used to worship Allah and proceed in the path of need and poverty without being a lord.

What is in the hands of the people of the books of the Gospel admits this, stating clearly that Mary was a young woman who believed in Allah and worshipped Him, and stating clearly that Jesus was born from her as a human from a human, and stating clearly that Jesus was a messenger from Allah to the people like the rest of the messengers, and stating clearly that Jesus and his mother Mary used to eat food.

These are matters stated clearly by the Gospels, and they are arguments for his (عليه السلام) being a servant and a messenger.

It is possible that the verse is directed toward negating the divinity of both the Messiah and his mother, as appears from His saying, the Exalted: **أَأَنْتَ قُلْتَ لِلنَّاسِ اتَّخِذُونِي وَأُمِّيَ إِلَٰهَيْنِ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ** — "‘Did you say to the people, ‘‘Take me and my mother for two gods besides Allah’’?’" (5:116), that there were those who spoke of her divinity like the Messiah. Or the intended meaning is taking her as a god as is attributed to the People of the Book that they took their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah, and that was by submitting to her and to them in a way that one does not submit to a human.

In any case, the verse according to this estimation negates divinity from the Messiah and his mother together by stating that the Messiah was a messenger like the rest of the messengers, and his mother was a truthful woman, and they both used to eat food, and all of that contradicts divinity.

In His saying, the Exalted: **قَدْ خَلَتْ مِنْ قَبْلِهِ الرُّسُلُ** — "Certainly [other] apostles have passed before him," where He described the messengers as having passed before him—which is death—there is an emphasis of the argument for his being a human for whom death and life are possible just as they were possible for the messengers before him.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **انْظُرْ كَيْفَ نُبَيِّنُ لَهُمُ الْآيَاتِ ثُمَّ انْظُرْ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ** — "Observe how We clarify the signs for them, and then observe how they are sided away!" (5:75). The discourse is to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), and it is in the position of expressing wonder (التعجيب); meaning: wonder at how We clarify the signs for them—and it is the clearest clarification of the most manifest sign of the falsehood of their claim of the Messiah's divinity—and how they are turned away from conceptualizing these signs. To what end are they turned away from them, such that their intellects do not pay attention to their result—which is the falsehood of their claim?

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا ۚ وَاللَّهُ هُوَ السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ** — "Say, ‘Do you worship besides Allah what has no power to bring you any benefit or harm? And Allah is the All-hearing, the All-knowing’" (5:76). Submission to the matter of Lordship (الربوبية) only spread among mankind in its earliest eras, and especially among the general public—and their general public used to worship idols—out of desire that the Lord would repel evil from them and bring benefit to them, as is obtained from historical research. As for the worship of Allah because He is Allah, exalted is His Name, it did not exceed the elite among them, such as the prophets and the godly ones (الربانيين) of their nations.

So Allah commanded His Messenger to address them with the discourse of the simple human, proceeding according to what his simple primordial nature (فطرته الساذجة) inspires him in the worship of Allah, just as He addressed the idolaters and the worshippers of idols with that. He reminds them that what compels a human to worship the Lord is that he sees the reins of good and evil, benefit and harm, in His hand; so he worships Him because He possesses harm and benefit, hoping that He will repel harm from him and bring good to him due to his worship of Him.

Everything that is besides Allah, the Exalted, does not possess anything of harm or benefit because it is purely owned by Allah, stripped of power in itself. How then is it permissible to single it out for worship and to associate it with his Lord, who is the Owner of it and of others? It was obligatory that He, the Exalted, be singled out for worship and that one does not overstep Him to another, because He is the one to whom hearing and responding are exclusive; so He hears and responds to the distressed when he calls upon Him. He is the one who knows the needs of His servants and is not heedless of them nor does He err in them, unlike others besides Him, the Exalted, for they only possess what Allah has given them to possess, and they are only capable of what Allah, the Glorified, has empowered them to do.

It has become clear by this clarification: **First:** that the argument contained in this verse is different from the argument contained in the previous verse, even if they both depend on a shared premise, which is the Messiah and his mother being contingent and needy. The previous verse's argument is that the Messiah and his mother were two humans, needy, servants, and obedient to Allah, the Glorified; and whoever's state is this state, it is not correct for him to be a worshipped god. The argument of this verse is: that the Messiah is a contingent, needy being, owned in himself, possessing neither harm nor benefit; and whoever's state is this state, his divinity and worship besides Allah are not upright.

**Second:** that the argument is taken from what the simple understanding and the naive intellect perceive from the perspective of the simple human's purpose in his worship. For he only takes a Lord and worships Him to repel harm from himself and bring benefit to himself, and this is only possessed by Allah, the Exalted, and not by others. Thus, no purpose relates to the worship of other than Allah, so it is obligatory to reject his worship.

**Third:** that His saying: **مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا** — "what has no power to bring you any benefit or harm," the word **مَا** (what) was used instead of the word "who" (من) despite the Messiah being among the possessors of intellect, because the argument itself is the one established against the idolaters and the worshippers of idols that have no consciousness. The fact that the Messiah (عليه السلام) is among the possessors of intellect has no role in the completion of the argument; it is complete regarding every supposed deity besides Allah, the Glorified.

Furthermore, others besides Him, the Exalted, even if they are among the possessors of intellect and consciousness, do not possess anything of intellect and consciousness from themselves, just like the rest of what is attributed to them of the affairs of their existence. He, the Exalted, said: **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ عِبَادٌ أَمْثَالُكُمْ ۖ فَادْعُوهُمْ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا لَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صَادِقِينَ * أَلَهُمْ أَرْجُلٌ يَمْشُونَ بِهَا ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَيْدٍ يَبْطِشُونَ بِهَا ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَا ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ آذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۗ قُلِ ادْعُوا شُرَكَاءَكُمْ ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ فَلَا تُنْظِرُونِ** — "Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah are creatures like yourselves. So invoke them: let them answer you, if you are truthful. Do they have feet with which they walk? Do they have hands with which they grasp? Do they have eyes with which they see? Do they have ears with which they hear? Say, ‘Invoke your partners [of Allah], then plot against me, and give me no respite’" (7:194-195).

Likewise, the precedence of harm over benefit in His saying: **ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا** — "harm or benefit" is to proceed in accordance with what the simple primordial nature perceives and calls toward, as mentioned. For a human, according to nature, sees the blessings he is clothed with and which are present with him as long as they are with him as owned by himself; his soul does not pay attention to the possibility of losing them, nor does it imagine its pain upon losing them. This is in contrast to the harms he finds in actuality, and the blessings he misses and finds the pain of missing; for the primordial nature alerts him to seek refuge in a Lord who repels harm and injury from him and brings to him the stripped blessing. As He, the Exalted, said: **وَإِذَا مَسَّ الْإِنْسَانَ الضُّرُّ دَعَانَا لِجَنْبِهِ أَوْ قَاعِدًا أَوْ قَائِمًا فَلَمَّا كَشَفْنَا عَنْهُ ضُرَّهُ مَرَّ كَأَنْ لَمْ يَدْعُنَا إِلَىٰ ضُرٍّ مَسَّهُ** — "When distress befalls man, he supplicates Us, [lying] on his side, or sitting, or standing; but when We remove his distress from him, he passes on as if he had never supplicated Us for any distress that had befallen him" (10:12). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَلَئِنْ أَذَقْنَاهُ رَحْمَةً مِنَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ضَرَّاءَ مَسَّتْهُ لَيَقُولَنَّ هَٰذَا لِي** — "And if We let him taste a mercy from Us after some adversity that has visited him, he will surely say, ‘This is my due’" (41:50). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَإِذَا أَنْعَمْنَا عَلَى الْإِنْسَانِ أَعْرَضَ وَنَأَىٰ بِجَانِبِهِ وَإِذَا مَسَّهُ الشَّرُّ فَذُو دُعَاءٍ عَرِيضٍ** — "When We bless man, he is disregardful and turns aside; but when an ill visits him, he is full of prolonged supplications" (41:51).

Thus, it is obtained that the touch of harm is more of an incentive for a human to submit to the Lord and worship Him than the finding of benefit. Therefore, Allah, the Glorified, gave precedence to harm over benefit in His saying: **مَا لَا يَمْلِكُ لَكُمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا**, and likewise in other similar instances, such as His saying: **اتَّخَذُوا مِنْ دُونِهِ آلِهَةً لَا يَخْلُقُونَ شَيْئًا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ لِأَنْفُسِهِمْ ضَرًّا وَلَا نَفْعًا وَلَا يَمْلِكُونَ مَوْتًا وَلَا حَيَاةً وَلَا نُشُورًا** — "They have taken gods besides Him who create nothing and are themselves created, and who have no power to bring benefit or harm to themselves, nor do they have any power over death, life, or resurrection" (25:3).

**Fourth:** that the entirety of the verse: **أَتَعْبُدُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ** until its end is an argument for the obligation of restricting worship to Allah, the Glorified, without associating anything else with Him. It is divisible into two arguments whose summary is: that the taking of a God and the worship of a Lord is only for the purpose of repelling harm and bringing benefit, so the worshipped God must be the owner of that, and it is not permissible to worship one who owns nothing. Allah, the Glorified, is the All-Hearing, the Responder to the call, the All-Knowing of the essence of the need without ignorance, unlike others besides Him; so it is obligatory to worship Him without associating another.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلْ يَا أَهْلَ الْكِتَابِ لَا تَغْلُوا فِي دِينِكُمْ غَيْرَ الْحَقِّ** — "Say, ‘O People of the Book! Do not exceed the bounds in your religion unduly’" (5:77). Another discourse to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) commanding him to call the People of the Book to not exaggerate (الغلو) in their religion. The People of the Book, and especially the Christians, are afflicted with that. The "exaggerator" (الغالي) is the one who oversteps the limit by excess (الإفراط), and its opposite is the "detractor" (القالي) on the side of deficiency (التفريط).

The religion of Allah, which His revealed books explain, commands monotheism (التوحيد) and the negation of the partner, and forbids the taking of partners for Allah, the Glorified. The People of the Book—the Jews and Christians generally—have been afflicted with this, even if the matter of the Christians in this is more hideous and more monstrous. He, the Exalted, said: **وَقَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ابْنُ اللَّهِ وَقَالَتِ النَّصَارَى الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ اللَّهِ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ قَوْلُهُمْ بِأَفْوَاهِهِمْ ۖ يُضَاهِئُونَ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ ۚ قَاتَلَهُمُ اللَّهُ ۚ أَنَّىٰ يُؤْفَكُونَ * اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَانَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَالْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ وَمَا أُمِرُوا إِلَّا لِيَعْبُدُوا إِلَٰهًا وَاحِدًا ۖ لَا إِلَٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ۚ سُبْحَانَهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** — "The Jews say, ‘Ezra is the son of Allah,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Messiah is the son of Allah.’ That is an opinion they mouth, imitating the opinions of the faithless of former times. May Allah assail them! Where do they stray? They have taken their scribes and their monks as lords besides Allah, and also the Messiah, son of Mary; though they were not commanded except to worship the One God: there is no god except Him. Immaculate is He from the partners they ascribe [to Him]!" (9:30-31).

The statement that Ezra is the son of Allah, even if it is not apparent today among the Jews, the verse testifies that they used to say that in the era of revelation.

It appears that this was an honorific title they gave him in exchange for what he served them and the good he did for them in returning them to Jerusalem (بيت المقدس) after the Babylonian captivity, and his collecting the Torah for them a second time after its loss in the story of Nebuchadnezzar. They used to consider sonship to Allah an honorific title, just as the Christians today take fatherhood as such and call the Popes, Patriarchs, and Priests "Fathers" (the Pope: the Father). And He, the Exalted, said: **وَقَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ وَالنَّصَارَىٰ نَحْنُ أَبْنَاءُ اللَّهِ وَأَحِبَّاؤُهُ** — "The Jews and the Christians say, ‘We are Allah’s children and His beloved ones’" (5:18).

Rather, the second verse—meaning His saying: **اتَّخَذُوا أَحْبَارَهُمْ وَرُهْبَانَهُمْ أَرْبَابًا مِنْ دُونِ اللَّهِ وَالْمَسِيحَ ابْنَ مَرْيَمَ**—indicates that, as it restricted the mention of the Messiah (عليه السلام) and did not mention Ezra, indicating his inclusion in the generality of His saying: "their scribes and their monks," and that they only used to call him the son of Allah as they call their scribes the sons of Allah. They singled him out for mention alone in gratitude for his kindness to them as previously alluded to.

In short, their placing some of their prophets, scribes, and monks in the position of Lordship and their submission to them in a way that one does not submit except to Allah, the Glorified, is an exaggeration (غلو) in their religion from which Allah forbids them through the tongue of His Prophet (ص).

The qualification of exaggeration in religion as being "unduly" (بغير الحق)—and exaggeration is never otherwise—is only for emphasis and to remind of the necessary meaning along with the necessitated one, so that the listener does not become heedless of it, as he was heedless when he exaggerated or was like one who is heedless.

The application of "Father" to Allah, the Glorified, by analyzing its meaning and stripping it of the mark of the deficiencies of physical matter—meaning the one in whose hand is origination and nurturing—and likewise "Son" in its abstract, analytical meaning, even if the intellect does not prohibit it, it is prohibited by the Law (شرعا) because the names of Allah, the Glorified, are restricted to those revealed (توقيفية). This is due to the corruptions (المفاسد) in the expansion of applying various names to Him, the Exalted. Sufficient as a corruption in the application of "Father" and "Son" is what the two nations—the Jews and Christians, and especially the Christians from the patrons of the Church—have encountered through long centuries, and the matter shall not cease to be so.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَا تَتَّبِعُوا أَهْوَاءَ قَوْمٍ قَدْ ضَلُّوا مِنْ قَبْلُ وَأَضَلُّوا كَثِيرًا وَضَلُّوا عَنْ سَوَاءِ السَّبِيلِ** — "and do not follow the desires of a people who have gone astray before, and led many astray, and strayed from the right way" (5:77). The apparent context is that the intended meaning of these people whose desires they were forbidden from following are the followed ones who are obeyed in their opinions and commands. Thus, their straying was due to their commitment to their opinions; their leading many astray was the following of others of them; and their straying from the right way is what resulted for them from their straying and leading astray, which is straying upon straying.

Likewise, the apparent context is that the intended meaning of them is the pagans and the worshippers of idols. For the apparent context is that the discourse is to all the People of the Book, not just those contemporary to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), such that it would be a prohibition for their later ones from following their earlier ones.

This is supported, rather indicated, by His saying, the Exalted: **وَقَالَتِ الْيَهُودُ عُزَيْرٌ ابْنُ اللَّهِ وَقَالَتِ النَّصَارَى الْمَسِيحُ ابْنُ اللَّهِ ۖ ذَٰلِكَ قَوْلُهُمْ بِأَفْوَاهِهِمْ ۖ يُضَاهِئُونَ قَوْلَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ قَبْلُ** — "The Jews say, ‘Ezra is the son of Allah,’ and the Christians say, ‘The Messiah is the son of Allah.’ That is an opinion they mouth, imitating the opinions of the faithless of former times" (9:30).

So that would be an analytical historical reality pointed to by the Noble Quran: that the statement of fatherhood and sonship is among what seeped into the People of the Book from those who preceded them of the pagans. It has already passed in the discussion on the stories of the Messiah (عليه السلام) in Surah Al-Imran in the third volume of the book that this statement, among a group of statements and opinions, is found among the Brahmanical and Buddhist pagans in India and China, and likewise ancient Egypt and others. It only began to seep into the community of the Book at the hands of its missionaries, so it appeared in the garb of religion; the name was for the religion of monotheism and the named was for paganism.

His saying, the Exalted: **لُعِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرَائِيلَ عَلَىٰ لِسَانِ دَاوُودَ وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ** — "The faithless among the Children of Israel were cursed by the tongue of David and Jesus, son of Mary" (5:78) until the end of the two verses. It is an information that the disbelievers among them are cursed by the tongues of their prophets. In it is an insinuation against these whom Allah has deemed disbelievers in these verses from the Jews, being cursed by the call of their own prophets, and that was because of their disobedience to their prophets, and they were persistent in transgression. His saying: **كَانُوا لَا يَتَنَاهَوْنَ عَنْ مُنْكَرٍ فَعَلُوهُ** — "They would not forbid one another from the wrongs they committed" (5:79) is a clarification of His saying: **وَكَانُوا يَعْتَدُونَ** — "and they used to transgress" (5:78).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **تَرَىٰ كَثِيرًا مِنْهُمْ يَتَوَلَّوْنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا** — "You see many of them befriending the faithless" (5:80) until the end. This is of the type of seeking testimony from the senses for their being transgressors. For if they had estimated their religion with its true worth, they would have adhered to it and not transgressed it. The necessity of that would be that they befriend the people of monotheism and disassociate from those who disbelieve, because the enemies of what a people sanctify are the enemies of that people. So if they love and befriend one another, it indicates the turning away of that people and their abandonment of what they used to sanctify and respect; and the friend of the enemy is an enemy. Then Allah censured them with His saying: **لَبِئْسَ مَا قَدَّمَتْ لَهُمْ أَنْفُسُهُمْ** — "Evil is what they have sent ahead for themselves" (5:80), which is the guardianship (ولاية) of the disbelievers out of the soul's whim. Its recompense and consequence was: **أَنْ سَخِطَ اللَّهُ عَلَيْهِمْ وَفِي الْعَذَابِ هُمْ خَالِدُونَ** — "that Allah is displeased with them and they shall remain in punishment [forever]" (5:80). In the verse, the recompense of the deed and its outcome is placed in the position of the deed, as if their souls sent ahead for them the recompense of the deed by sending ahead the deed itself.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَلَوْ كَانُوا يُؤْمِنُونَ بِاللَّهِ وَالنَّبِيِّ وَمَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْهِ مَا اتَّخَذُوهُمْ أَوْلِيَاءَ وَلَٰكِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِنْهُمْ فَاسِقُونَ** — "Had they believed in Allah and the Prophet and what was sent down to him, they would not have taken them for friends, but many of them are transgressors" (5:81). That is, if these People of the Book believed in Allah and the Prophet Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and what was sent down to him, or the prophet of themselves such as Moses for example and what was sent down to him such as the Torah for example, they would not have taken those disbelievers as friends; for faith (الإيمان) cuts off all other causes. But many of them are transgressors (فاسقون), rebellious against faith.

In the verse is another possibility they have suggested, which is that the pronouns in His saying **كَانُوا** (they were), **يُؤْمِنُونَ** (they believe), and **اتَّخَذُوهُمْ** (they took them) in His saying **مَا اتَّخَذُوهُمْ** return to the disbelievers. The meaning would be: "Had those disbelievers—those disbelievers whom the People of the Book befriend—believed in Allah, the Prophet, and the Quran, the People of the Book would not have taken them as friends." They only befriended them because of their disbelief. This is a plausible view, except that the transition (الإضراب) in His saying: **وَلَٰكِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِنْهُمْ فَاسِقُونَ** does not suit it.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **لَتَجِدَنَّ أَشَدَّ النَّاسِ عَدَاوَةً لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا الْيَهُودَ وَالَّذِينَ أَشْرَكُوا** — "Surely you will find the most hostile of people toward the faithful to be the Jews and the polytheists" until His saying: **نَصَارَىٰ** — "Christians" (5:82). When He, the Glorified, clarified in the previous verses the vices shared among the People of the Book generally, and some of what is specific to some of them—such as the saying of the Jews: "Allah’s hand is tied up" and the saying of the Christians: "Indeed Allah is the Messiah, son of Mary"—He concluded the verses with what is specific to each of the two groups when their state is compared to the believers and their religion. He added to their state the state of the polytheists (المشركين) so that the discourse would be complete regarding the impact of Islam upon the hearts of the non-Muslim nations in terms of their proximity and distance from accepting it.

The discourse is completed in that the Christians are the closest of those nations in affection (مودة) to the Muslims and the most attentive to their true call.

Allah, the Glorified, only counted them as the closest in affection to the Muslims because of the faith of a group of them in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), as indicated by His saying in the following verse: **وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا مَا أُنْزِلَ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ** — "When they hear what has been sent down to the Apostle" (5:83). However, if the faith of a group made this attribution to all of them correct, it would have been obligatory to count the Jews and the polytheists like the Christians and attribute to them something similar to what was attributed to them, because of the Islam of a group of the Jews such as Abdullah bin Salam and his companions, and the Islam of many of the Arab polytheists—who are the generality of the Muslims today. Thus, the singling out of the Christians with the like of His saying: **وَإِذَا سَمِعُوا مَا أُنْزِلَ** until the end, excluding the Jews and the polytheists, indicates their good reception of the Islamic call and the response to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), despite their being at a choice between remaining upon their religion and paying the tribute (الجزية), or accepting Islam, or fighting.

This is in contrast to the polytheists, for nothing was accepted from them except the acceptance of the call; thus the multiplicity of believers among them does not indicate a good response, considering what the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) suffered from their harshness and what the Muslims encountered from their hands of cruelty and arrogance.

Likewise, the Jews, even if they were like the Christians in the possibility of their remaining upon their religion and paying the tribute to the Muslims, they persisted in their arrogance, hardened in their partisanship (عصبيتهم), took to plotting and deception, broke their covenants, and waited for misfortunes to befall the Muslims, touching them with the most painful and distressing matters.

This which occurred of the matter of the Christians with the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and the Islamic call, and their good response, and likewise of the matter of the Jews and the polytheists in persisting in arrogance and partisanship, occurred exactly the same after him (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) following the pattern of what occurred in his era. How many have answered the Islamic call from the sects of the Christians during the past centuries, and how few has that been from the Jews and the pagans! Thus, the preservation of this characteristic in these and those confirms the Mighty Book in what it provided.

It is known that His saying, the Exalted: **لَتَجِدَنَّ أَشَدَّ النَّاسِ عَدَاوَةً لِلَّذِينَ آمَنُوا** is of the type of clarifying a general rule (الضابط العام) in the form of a specific discourse, similar to what passed in the previous verses: "You see many of them befriending the faithless" and "You see many of them hastening in sin."

His saying, the Exalted: **ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّ مِنْهُمْ قِسِّيسِينَ وَرُهْبَانًا وَأَنَّهُمْ لَا يَسْتَكْبِرُونَ** — "That is because there are priests and monks among them, and because they are not arrogant" (5:82). The priest (القسيس) is the Arabized form of *kashish*. The monks (الرهبان) is the plural of monk (الراهب), and it may be singular. Al-Raghib said: "Fear (الرهبة) and dread (الرهب) is fear with caution—until he said—and *al-tarahhub* is worship, and monasticism (الرهبانية) is exaggeration in enduring worship out of excessive fear. He, the Exalted, said: **وَرَهْبَانِيَّةً ابْتَدَعُوهَا** — ‘and monasticism which they innovated’ (57:27). And *al-ruhban* can be singular or plural; so whoever makes it singular, its plural is *rahabin*." End quote.

The Exalted has justified His mention of the Christians being nearest in affection and most sociable in heart toward those who believe by three characteristics lacked by others among the Jews and polytheists: that among them are scholars, that among them are monks and ascetics, and that they are not arrogant—this being the key to their readiness for felicity (السعادة).

This is because the felicity of the religious life consists in performing righteous deeds (صالح العمل) based on knowledge (العلم) thereof. Or, if you prefer, you could say: it is to submit to the truth (الحق) and then apply one's actions to it. Thus, one has a need for knowledge to perceive the truth of religion—which is the religion of truth (دين الحق). However, the mere perception of truth is not sufficient for one to be prepared to act according to it until the human being uproots from his soul the disposition that prevents it, namely, arrogance (الاستكبار) against the truth due to prejudice (عصبية) or the like. When a person clothes himself in beneficial knowledge (العلم النافع) and fairness (النصفة) toward the truth by removing arrogance, he becomes prepared to submit to the truth by acting upon it.

However, this is conditional upon the environment (الجو) not being contrary to it, for the conformity of the environment to action has a profound effect in the realm of deeds. Indeed, those deeds that are common to the society as a whole, in which its members are raised and upon which their habits are established from generation to generation, leave no room for the soul to reflect upon its affairs or to deliberate and plan an escape from them if they are harmful and destructive to felicity. Likewise is the case with righteous deeds; if being clothed in them becomes established in a society, it becomes difficult for the soul to abandon them. Hence it is said: "Habit is a second nature." For this reason, also, the first contrary act is extremely difficult (حرجاً) for the soul, as it serves as proof of possibility to the soul; thereafter, as each act is realized, the ease of realization increases and the difficulty decreases proportionately.

Thus, when a person realizes that such-and-such an act is a righteous truth and removes from his soul the motives of obstinacy (العناد) and persistence in contention (اللجاج) by putting to death arrogance and seeking superiority (الاستعلاء) over the truth, it is a great help for him to see another person performing it, for then his soul perceives the possibility of the action.

From here it becomes clear that a society is only prepared to accept the truth if it contains scholars (علماء) who know it and teach it, and men who stand to act upon it so that the commonality may submit to the possibility of the action and witness its beauty, and if their commonality is accustomed to submitting to the truth and not being arrogant toward it when it is revealed to them.

For this reason, Allah, the Glorified, justified the Christians' proximity to accepting the true religious call by the fact that among them are priests and monks and that they are not arrogant. Thus, among them are scholars who constantly remind them of the station of truth and the gnoses of religion (معارف الدين) through speech (قولاً), and among them are ascetics (زهاد) who remind them of the greatness of their Lord and the importance of their otherworldly and worldly felicity through action (عملاً), and among them is an absence of arrogance toward accepting the truth.

As for the Jews, although there were rabbis (أحبار) and scholars among them, they were arrogant; the vice of obstinacy and seeking superiority would not allow them to be prepared to accept the truth. As for those who associate partners with Allah (الذين أشركوا), they lack both scholars and ascetics, and within them is the vice of arrogance.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَإِذا سَمِعُوا ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَى الرَّسُولِ تَرى أَعْيُنَهُمْ تَفِيضُ مِنَ الدَّمْعِ** — "When they hear what has been sent down to the Apostle, you see their eyes overflow with tears" (5:83). The eye "overflowed" (فاضت) with tears means its tears flowed copiously. The preposition "from" (مِنَ) in His saying "from tears" (مِنَ الدَّمْعِ) denotes the beginning (الابتداء); in His saying "from what" (مِمَّا) it denotes the origin (النشوء); and in His saying "of the truth" (مِنَ الْحَقِّ) it is explanatory (بيانية).

**His saying, the Exalted:** **وَما لَنا لا نُؤْمِنُ بِاللهِ** — "Why should we not believe in Allah" (5:84). The word "admit us" (يُدْخِلَنا) is as if it implies the meaning of "placing" (الجعل), which is why it is made transitive with the preposition "with" (مع). The meaning is: "Our Lord places us with the righteous people, granting us entry among them."

In these actions and words that Allah the Exalted has recounted from them, there is a confirmation of what He mentioned regarding them being nearest in affection to those who believe, and a realization that among them is beneficial knowledge, righteous deeds, and submission to the truth, since there were among them priests and monks and they were not arrogant.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **فَأَثابَهُمُ اللهُ** — "So Allah rewarded them" (5:85) until the end of the two verses. "Reward" (الإثابة) is recompense (المجازاة). The first verse mentions their reward, and the second verse mentions the reward of those who opposed them by way of contrast, to complete the categories.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Ma'ani al-Akhbar*, with its chain of transmission from Imam al-Rida from his forefathers, from Imam Ali (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of the Exalted: **كانا يَأْكُلانِ الطَّعامَ** — "They both used to eat food" (5:75)—it means they both used to defecate.

**I say:** Al-Ayyashi also narrated it in his *Tafsir* as a *marfu'* tradition.

In *al-Kafi*, with its chain of transmission from Abu Ubaydah al-Hadhdha', from Imam Abu Abdillah (peace be upon him): regarding the saying of Allah, the Mighty and Majestic: **لُعِنَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا مِنْ بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ عَلى لِسانِ داوُدَ وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ** — "The faithless among the Children of Israel were cursed by the tongue of David and Jesus son of Mary" (5:78)—he said: "The swine were cursed by the tongue of David, and the apes by the tongue of Jesus son of Mary."

**I say:** It was narrated by al-Qummi and al-Ayyashi from him (peace be upon him). It is also narrated through Sunni chains from Mujahid, Qatadah, and others that the apes were cursed by the tongue of David and the swine by the tongue of Jesus son of Mary; some Shi'i narrations agree with this, as will follow.

In *al-Majma'*, from Imam Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him): "As for David, he cursed the people of Aylah when they transgressed in their Sabbath, and their transgression was in his time. He said: 'O Allah, clothe them in the curse like a cloak, and like a belt upon the waist.' So Allah transformed them into apes. As for Jesus, he cursed those to whom the Table (المائدة) was sent down—then they disbelieved after that." He said: Imam Abu Ja'far (peace be upon him) said: "They take as allies the tyrannical kings and beautify their desires for them so that they may obtain something of their world."

**I say:** The Quran supports the fact that the People of the Sabbath were transformed into apes. The Exalted said: **وَلَقَدْ عَلِمْتُمُ الَّذِينَ اعْتَدَوْا مِنْكُمْ فِي السَّبْتِ فَقُلْنا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً خاسِئِينَ** — "And certainly you know those of you who transgressed on the Sabbath, whereupon We said to them, ‘Be you spurned apes’" (2:65). And the Exalted said: **وَسْئَلْهُمْ عَنِ الْقَرْيَةِ الَّتِي كانَتْ حاضِرَةَ الْبَحْرِ إِذْ يَعْدُونَ فِي السَّبْتِ إِذْ تَأْتِيهِمْ حِيتانُهُمْ يَوْمَ سَبْتِهِمْ شُرَّعاً وَيَوْمَ لا يَسْبِتُونَ لا تَأْتِيهِمْ** — "Ask them about the town that was situated on the seaside, when they transgressed on the Sabbath, when their fish would come to them on the day of their Sabbath, visibly on the shore, but on days when they were not keeping Sabbath they would not come to them..." until He said: **وَإِذْ قالَتْ أُمَّةٌ مِنْهُمْ لِمَ تَعِظُونَ قَوْماً اللهُ مُهْلِكُهُمْ أَوْ مُعَذِّبُهُمْ عَذاباً شَدِيداً قالُوا مَعْذِرَةً إِلى رَبِّكُمْ وَلَعَلَّهُمْ يَتَّقُونَ** — "When a group of them said, ‘Why do you advise a people whom Allah will destroy or punish with a severe punishment?’ They said, ‘As an excuse before your Lord, and that they may be God-conscious’" until He said: **فَلَمَّا عَتَوْا عَنْ ما نُهُوا عَنْهُ قُلْنا لَهُمْ كُونُوا قِرَدَةً خاسِئِينَ** — "So when they defied what they had been forbidden, We said to them, ‘Be you spurned apes’" (7:163-166).

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*: Abd ibn Humayd, Abu al-Shaykh, al-Tabarani, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Mas'ud who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "When the Children of Israel committed sins, their scholars forbade them as a reprimand (تعزيراً), but then they sat with them, ate with them, and drank with them as if they had not committed a sin the day before. When Allah saw that from them, He struck the hearts of some against others and cursed them by the tongue of one of the prophets." Then the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "By Allah, you shall surely enjoin what is right (المعروف) and forbid what is wrong (المنكر), and you shall surely compel them toward the truth (الحق)—or Allah will surely strike the hearts of some of you against others and curse you as He cursed them."

Also in it: Abd ibn Humayd narrated from Mu'adh ibn Jabal who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Take the gift (العطاء) as long as it is a gift, but if it becomes a bribe (رشوة) for your religion, do not take it—though you will not leave it, for poverty and fear will prevent you from that. Indeed, the sons of Gog have come, and the millstone of Islam shall rotate; so wherever the Quran turns, turn with it. Soon the Sultan and the Quran shall fight and separate. Indeed, there will be kings over you who will judge for you with one judgment and for themselves with another. If you obey them, they will lead you astray, and if you disobey them, they will kill you."

They said: "O Messenger of Allah, what should we do if we reach that time?" He said: "Be like the companions of Jesus; they were sawn with saws and raised upon wooden beams. Death in obedience is better than life in disobedience. Indeed, the first thing that decreased among the Children of Israel was that they used to enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong as a matter of reprimand (سنة التعزير). A man would meet his companion whom he used to criticize, yet he would eat and drink with him as if he had not criticized him for anything. So Allah cursed them by the tongue of David, and that was because they disobeyed and used to transgress. By Him in Whose hand is my soul, you shall surely enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong, or Allah will surely set the worst of you over you; then the best of you will supplicate and they will not be answered. By Him in Whose hand is my soul, you shall surely enjoin what is right and forbid what is wrong, and you shall surely take the hand of the wrongdoer and compel him toward the truth—or Allah will surely strike your hearts against one another."

Also in it: Ibn Rahwayh, al-Bukhari in *al-Wuhdaniyyat*, Ibn al-Sakan, Ibn Mandah, and al-Bawardi in *Ma'rifat al-Sahabah*, al-Tabarani, Abu Nu'aym, and Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Abza, from his father:

He said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) delivered a sermon, praised Allah and lauded Him, then mentioned groups of Muslims and praised them well. Then he said: "What is the matter with people who do not teach their neighbors, nor give them understanding (يفقهونهم), nor enjoin them, nor forbid them? And what is the matter with people who do not learn from their neighbors, nor seek understanding, nor take heed? By Him in Whose hand is my soul, they shall surely teach their neighbors, or seek understanding, or take heed, or I will surely hasten punishment for them in the abode of this world." Then he descended and entered his house. The companions of the Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Whom does he mean by this speech?" They said: "We do not know him to mean anyone by this speech except the Ash'arites; they are jurists and scholars, and they have neighbors who are coarse and ignorant."

A group of Ash'arites gathered and entered upon the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and said: "You mentioned groups of Muslims with good, and you mentioned us with evil; what is our case?" The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "You shall surely teach your neighbors, give them understanding, enjoin them, and forbid them—or I will surely hasten punishment for them in the abode of this world." They said: "O Messenger of Allah, grant us a year's respite, for in a year we can teach them and they can learn."
